The surgeon uses a number one V-lock suture to sequentially take bites along the dome of the hernia defect, effectively closing and obliterating the dead space.

The surgeon places the mesh into the preperitoneal space, securing it with cardinal tacks and sutures at the lower aspect.
So now I have to decide what to do with this small midline epigastric incisional hernia.
The surgeon dissects and removes portions of the falciform ligament to ensure adequate coverage for the mesh placement.
The surgeon decides to perform an Intraperitoneal Onlay Mesh (IPOM) technique in the area of the small midline epigastric incisional hernia.
The surgeon primarily closes the hernia defect using a running OV lock suture technique, ensuring the edges of the defect are approximated securely.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap and secures the mesh while simultaneously closing the lower margin of the epigastric defect.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-lock suture to re-peritonealize the mesh in a canal-like fashion, securing it within the surgical field.
The resident buries a long, exposed V-lock suture within the surgical field to ensure it is not visible on the surface.
The surgeon uses a 13 by 15 centimeter sheet of Ventralite ST mesh, which is circumferentially sutured in place with a number one running V-Lock suture.
The surgeon resects the large hernia sac and closes the defect using a purse-string-like suture technique with a 3-0 V-Lock suture.
The patient was admitted for observation and was discharged on post update one.
